section: Tailoring Strength and Ductility of an Fe–Mn–Al–C Low-Density Duplex Steel by Controlling the Cooling Path after Hot Rolling

context_update.summary: This chunk introduces the Fe–8.03Mn–6.10Al–0.4C (wt%) low-density duplex steel and its processing route: vacuum induction melting, forging, homogenization at 1050 °C for 2 h, reheating at 1200 °C for 2 h, hot rolling between 900 and 1100 °C (seven passes to 4 mm), followed by either air cooling (AC) or furnace cooling (FC). Annealing is performed at 800, 900, or 1000 °C for 30 min, yielding sample aliases such as AC-A800, AC-A900, AC-A1000 and similarly for FC. Initial microstructural findings indicate that AC annealed samples consist of ferrite and austenite, while FC annealed samples contain banded ferrite, austenite, and martensite. Austenite stability in AC material is enhanced by grain refinement from needle-like α-ferrite. FC samples suffer from martensite‑induced cracks leading to premature fracture, whereas AC samples exhibit good ductility and a TRIP effect that causes work hardening rate recovery at high strain, resulting in a superior strength‑ductility balance. Characterization tools include SEM (nital etching), EBSD (20 kV, 0.1 μm step, electropolished), XRD, and uniaxial tensile tests (10⁻³ s⁻¹, 50 mm gauge length). Figure 1 shows the overall processing schedule. The next chunks are expected to detail microstructural evolution during annealing and the resulting tensile properties, work hardening behavior, and fracture features.
microstructure: FC annealed: banded ferrite + austenite + martensite
needle‑like α‑ferrite refines austenite grains in AC condition
properties: AC annealed: high ductility, TRIP effect, work hardening rate recovery, good strength‑ductility match
FC annealed: brittle fracture at lower strain due to martensite and banded structure
mechanisms: austenite stability improved by grain refinement via α‑ferrite
TRIP effect at later deformation stage

context_update.summary: This chunk provides tensile test parameters (10^-3 s^-1, gauge 50×12.5 mm, rolling direction) and XRD sample preparation. The main content covers the annealed microstructure evolution of Fe–8.03Mn–6.10Al–0.4C duplex steel. Hot-rolled AC exhibited 42% γ, 26% twin martensite with KAM 0.48°, while FC had 51% γ, 2.8% martensite, KAM 0.43°. After annealing at 800–1000 °C for 30 min, AC samples developed duplex ferrite + austenite with needle-like α-ferrite refined austenite grains (3.5–5.8 μm) and austenite fractions of 58.6–61.4% (XRD). The needle-like α-ferrite originated from partial martensite degeneration and, together with C/Mn partitioning, enhanced austenite chemical and mechanical stability, yielding Ms_lim of 1–34 °C and no martensite. FC annealed samples retained banded ferrite and austenite with larger grains (8.1–10.1 μm), some equiaxed α-ferrite, and minor martensite. Low-angle grain boundary lengths in ferrite indicated recovery progression. Figures 2–8 document all features. Subsequent sections should link these microstructures to tensile properties, work hardening behaviour, TRIP effect, and fracture features, using austenite fractions (including post-deformation data in Figure 5) and grain sizes for property models.
processing_route: air cooling or furnace cooling after rolling
microstructure: hot-rolled AC: 42% austenite, 26% twin martensite, δ-ferrite
AC annealed: duplex ferrite + austenite, needle-like α-ferrite, γ grain 3.5–5.8 μm
FC annealed: banded, γ grain 8.1–10.1 μm, small martensite, equiaxed α-ferrite
low-angle grain boundary lengths in ferrite: AC800 4351.1 μm, AC900 2685.4 μm, AC1000 1634.3 μm
mechanisms: austenite stability enhanced by needle-like α-ferrite grain refinement and C/Mn partitioning during annealing
martensite reversal to austenite and degeneration to α-ferrite
low stability in FC due to larger γ grain size leads to martensite during cooling

context_update.summary: This chunk presents tensile mechanical properties and deformation/fracture mechanisms of the Fe–8.03Mn–6.10Al–0.4C duplex steel after hot rolling and annealing. AC-rolled sample shows higher strength but lower ductility than FC. AC-annealed samples achieve higher yield strength (427–513 MPa) than FC-annealed (267–419 MPa) due to grain refinement (austenite grain size from 8–10 µm to 3–6 µm) and dislocation strengthening. Annealed AC samples all exhibit >30% elongation (e.g., AC-A800: 56.9%), while FC-annealed <20% (FC-A800: 19.0%). Work hardening curves show that AC-annealed samples develop a Stage III with recovered work hardening rate, enabling high strength–ductility synergy, whereas FC-annealed samples fracture abruptly without necking. TRIP effect is critical: ~60% austenite transforms in AC-A800 and drives Stage III hardening. FC-A800 shows cleavage fracture from cracks nucleating at martensite–austenite interfaces. AC-A800 fracture surface exhibits dimples indicating high ductility. The stable duplex ferrite + austenite from AC+annealing is recommended as a practical route. Figures 9, 10, and 11 document engineering stress–strain curves, work hardening rate curves, and fractographs. Subsequent chunk may discuss additional property details or extend the deformation mechanisms.
properties: Stage III work hardening recovery for AC-annealed
mechanisms: TRIP effect from austenite transformation (~60% in AC-A800)
crack nucleation at martensite–austenite interfaces in FC samples
cleavage fracture in FC, ductile dimple fracture in AC

context_update.summary: This chunk concludes the study and reiterates the microstructural evolution during annealing for air-cooled (AC) and furnace-cooled (FC) conditions. AC hot-rolled sample contains δ-ferrite, austenite, and martensite; annealing partially transforms martensite to austenite and needle-like α-ferrite, yielding a final duplex δ-ferrite + needle-like α-ferrite + austenite structure. In contrast, FC annealing only coarsens austenite and forms slight equiaxed α-ferrite, with post-annealing martensite formation due to larger austenite grains, resulting in δ-ferrite + equiaxed α-ferrite + martensite + austenite. The AC-annealed microstructure enables a better strength-ductility synergy via Stage III work hardening recovery driven by a significant TRIP effect, while FC-annealed material fails by cleavage-dominated brittle fracture with cracks at martensite–austenite interfaces and delamination of banded structure. Figures 2 and 9 are referenced to support the strength-ductility synergy.
mechanisms: reverse transformation of martensite to austenite
TRIP effect enabling work hardening recovery

Subsequent chunk may discuss additional property details or extend the deformation mechanisms. This chunk concludes the study and reiterates the microstructural evolution during annealing for air-cooled (AC) and furnace-cooled (FC) conditions. AC hot-rolled sample contains δ-ferrite, austenite, and martensite; annealing partially transforms martensite to austenite and needle-like α-ferrite, yielding a final duplex δ-ferrite + needle-like α-ferrite + austenite structure. In contrast, FC annealing only coarsens austenite and forms slight equiaxed α-ferrite, with post-annealing martensite formation due to larger austenite grains, resulting in δ-ferrite + equiaxed α-ferrite + martensite + austenite. The AC-annealed microstructure enables a better strength-ductility synergy via Stage III work hardening recovery driven by a significant TRIP effect, while FC-annealed material fails by cleavage-dominated brittle fracture with cracks at martensite–austenite interfaces and delamination of banded structure. Figures 2 and 9 are referenced to support the strength-ductility synergy.
